coredata.dc:title: Preparation of surface-active carbon dots with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains via a one-pot solvothermal technique and their characterization and potential applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yan
Zhuang, Shuang
Chen, Wei-Guang
Lu, Lei
Li, Rong-Zhen
Chen, Pei-Yao
Chen, Yu
coredata.dc:description: Amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) are new surface-active materials that exhibit several advantages over conventional small surfactants, such as improved stability and reduced toxicity. However, unlike for conventional small surfactants, amphiphilic CDs that simultaneously exhibit excellent surface and interfacial activities have never been achieved. Herein we prepare new amphiphilic CDs with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid hydrophobic chains (FH-CDs) using a facile one-pot solvothermal technique. The obtained FH-CDs showed excellent surface and interfacial activities, and reduced the surface tension at critical micelle concentration and the oil-water interfacial tension to 16.7 and 2.2 mN/m, respectively. Comparison with control CDs bearing only non-hybrid hydrophobic chains demonstrated that decorating CDs with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains contributed to the excellent interfacial activity, while only fluorocarbon chains contributed to good surface activity. The excellent surface and interfacial activities made FH-CDs good emulsifiers for the formation of stable water-in-oil emulsions and for the preparation of relatively uniform polystyrene spheres via emulsion polymerization. Moreover, FH-CD was a good wetting agent, whose aqueous solution could wet numerous different surfaces, including the commonly non-wettable amphiphobic polytetrafluoroethylene.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amphiphilic carbon dots
Emulsifiers
Fluorocarbon
Interfacial activity
Surface activity
Wetting agents

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Emulsion polymerization with FH-CD as an emulsifier
Surface and interfacial activities of FH-CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Key Research and Development Program of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China ( 2022YFC3004900 ). We thank Liwen Bianji (Edanz) ( www.liwenbianji.cn ) for editing the English text of a draft of this manuscript.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence spectra of FH-CDs prepared under the different solvothermal conditions: (A) different reaction temperatures for 6 h and (B) different times at 140 °C.
γ versus log C for FH-CD under different reaction conditions: (A) temperature and (B) time (FH-CD3 shown in Table 1 as the representative).
Typical (A) TEM image, (B) FT-IR spectrum, (C) 1H NMR spectrum and (D) 19F NMR spectrum of FH-CD.
Typical XPS spectra: (A) full spectrum, (B) C1s, (C) O1s, and (D) F1s of FH-CDs.

Comparison of CMC and γ
CMC of (A) FH-CDs with different-length hydrocarbon chains, (B) FH-CDs with different ratios of F13-OH/n-octane alcohol, (C) FH-CD, F-CD, and H-CD, and (D) FH-CD and commercially available C1157 and C1460.
Typical plots of γ versus log C for FH-CD at different temperatures (FH-CD3 was used as the representative).

Typical plots of γ o/w versus C for (A) FH-CDs with different length hydrocarbon chains, (B) FH-CDs with different ratios of F13-OH/n-octane alcohol, (C) FH-CD, F-CD, and H-CD, and (D) FH-CD and commercially available C1157 and C1460.

(A) Photographs of the oil-water binary mixtures before and after emulsification with pure CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, F-CD, and FH-CD; and the corresponding optical microscopy images of the formed emulsions in the presence of (B) pure CD, (C) PEG-CD, (D) H-CD, (E) F-CD, and (F) FH-CD.

(A) Optical microscopy image of the PS emulsion (inset: photograph of the PS emulsion and the corresponding fluorescence image), (B, C) SEM images of PS latexes with different amplification times, and the corresponding EDS images of (D) C, (E) O, and (F) F.
SEM images of PS latexes prepared by styrene emulsion polymerization in the presence of different emulsifiers with a concentration of 1 mg/mL (inset: photographs of the PS emulsions).
Photographs of one drop of aqueous solutions of CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, F-CD, and FH-CD on different substance surfaces (Concentration increases from 5 to 12 mg/mL).
Preparation and application of water-soluble FH-CDs.
Formulations for preparing FH-CDs and their resulting molecular weights and dispersities.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a) Measured by GPC calibrated with PMMA standards; b) Đ is the dispersity measured from GPC

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Surface properties and thermodynamic parameters of FH-CDs in water at different temperatures.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: a) C 20 denotes the surfactant concentration required to reduce the surface tension of water by 20 mN/m, and pC 20 = - lgC 20

head.ce:title: Preparation of surface-active carbon dots with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains via a one-pot solvothermal technique and their characterization and potential applications

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, School of Science, Tianjin University, Tianjin 300354, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tianjin Fire Research Institute of MEM, Tianjin 300381, P. R. China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Amphiphilic carbon dots (CDs) are new surface-active materials that exhibit several advantages over conventional small surfactants, such as improved stability and reduced toxicity. However, unlike for conventional small surfactants, amphiphilic CDs that simultaneously exhibit excellent surface and interfacial activities have never been achieved. Herein we prepare new amphiphilic CDs with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid hydrophobic chains (FH-CDs) using a facile one-pot solvothermal technique. The obtained FH-CDs showed excellent surface and interfacial activities, and reduced the surface tension at critical micelle concentration and the oil-water interfacial tension to 16.7 and 2.2 mN/m, respectively. Comparison with control CDs bearing only non-hybrid hydrophobic chains demonstrated that decorating CDs with fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains contributed to the excellent interfacial activity, while only fluorocarbon chains contributed to good surface activity. The excellent surface and interfacial activities made FH-CDs good emulsifiers for the formation of stable water-in-oil emulsions and for the preparation of relatively uniform polystyrene spheres via emulsion polymerization. Moreover, FH-CD was a good wetting agent, whose aqueous solution could wet numerous different surfaces, including the commonly non-wettable amphiphobic polytetrafluoroethylene.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Surfactants bearing both hydrophilic and hydrophobic units are characteristic of the ability to reduce both surface and interface tension. They can generate a series of effects such as wetting, emulsifying, foaming, solubilizing, and dispersing [1], leading to their application in various fields, including washing, textiles, petroleum, firefighting, coating, pesticides, and pharmaceuticals [2–7]. The most widely used surfactants are those bearing both polar head-groups and nonpolar hydrocarbon tails, known as hydrocarbon surfactants. A special kind of surfactant known as fluorocarbon surfactants or fluorosurfactants have polar head-groups similarly to those of hydrocarbon surfactants, but different fluorocarbon tails [8]. In addition to the conventional small surfactants with one or two fluorocarbon tails, polymeric surfactants with many fluorocarbon side-chains have been developed [9–12]. They are bigger size with more versatile complex structure and exhibit several advantages over conventional small surfactants, such as improved stability and reduced toxicity [13–15].

With the development of nanotechnology, low cost and easily modified amphiphilic nanoparticles have gradually become novel surface-active materials [16–23] that have numerous similarities to polymeric surfactants, but whose dimensions can be modulated more facilely across a wider range. Among these amphiphilic nanoparticles, amphiphilic carbon materials, such as carbon dots (CDs) have attracted significant attention owing to their superior optical properties, facile preparation, having a wide range of raw materials, low toxicity, low environmental impact, and good biocompatibility [24–28]. Most studies have used the amphiphilic CDs for detection, analysis, bioimaging, and drug/gene delivery [29–32]. Recently however, amphiphilic CDs have attracted considerable interest as efficient surface-active materials as they can produce highly stable emulsions [33–36]. Excellent surface and interfacial activities are always targets when developing novel surface-active materials. However, current amphiphilic nanoparticles have not exhibited good enough surface and interfacial activities (see Table S1), which limits their potential application range. For example, hydrocarbon oils or hydrophobic plastics, such as polyethylene and polypropylene (PP), generally have a surface tension of approximately 25 mN/m. The surface tension of amphiphobic polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) is below 20 mN/m. For efficient coverage, the total surface and interfacial tensions should be less than those of the target materials [37]. To the best of our knowledge, no amphiphilic nanoparticles with surface tension below 20 mN/m have been reported to date, thus the relatively lower surface and interfacial activities of current amphiphilic nanoparticles make them unable to wet the above materials with low surface energy. Herein, we prepared water-soluble amphiphilic CDs with both fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon chains (FH-CDs) via a facile one-pot solvothermal method. It was found that the surface tensions of the as-prepared FH-CDs were as low as 16.7 mN/m and their oil-water interfacial tension was as low as ∼2.2 mN/m. The excellent surface and interfacial activities made FH-CDs suitable for emulsifying and wetting applications.

Polyethylene glycol monomethyl ethers (mPEG) with number average molecular weight (M n) of 750 and 2000 Da were purchased from Tianjin Heowns Biochemical Company. Perfluorohexyl ethyl alcohol (F13-OH) was purchased from Jinan Jinbang Environmental Protection Technology Co., Ltd. Citric acid (CA) and styrene were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. n-Hexanol, n-heptanol, n-octanol, and N,N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) were purchased from Tianjin Jiang Tian Chemical Technology Company. Only the main reagents are listed here, the others are detailed in the Supporting Information.

The novel FH-CDs were synthesized using a facile one-pot solvothermal method. It is exemplified with the FH-CD3 sample (see Table 1): 10 g of CA, 15 g of mPEG, 15 g of F13-OH, and 15 g of n-octanol were mixed with 120 mL of DMF to form a transparent solution. The solution was then transferred to a 250-mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated in an oven at 140 °C for 6 h. The mixture was dialyzed in methanol with 500-Da dialysis membrane for 24 h. Subsequently, the solvents were removed under vacuum and yellowish oily FH-CDs were obtained. For other samples, the amount of CA, mPEG, F13-OH, and DMF were kept constant, while the ratio of F13-OH to fatty alcohol or the length of hydrocarbon chain were varied. The yields of the obtained FH-CDs were all approximately 60 %.

Surface and interfacial tension (cyclohexane) measurements were carried out on a Sigma 700 automatic surface tensiometer (Biolin Scientific) at 25 °C using the Du Nouy Ring method. Before each measurement, the surface tensiometer was calibrated with distilled water. Each measurement was repeated at least three times.

Aqueous solutions of surfactants (5 mg/mL) were mixed with cyclohexane at a volume ratio of 1:1. After shaking vigorously for 1 min, the mixture was allowed to settle at room temperature for the formation of a stable emulsion in the cyclohexane layer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Emulsion polymerization with FH-CD as an emulsifier
ce:section.ce:para: Styrene polymerization was carried out by dispersing FH-CDs and styrene in deionized water, at concentrations of 15 mg/mL and 0.3 g/mL, respectively. After sonicating in an ice water bath for 20 min, the temperature was raised to 75 °C under argon protection. Then, 6 mg/mL of the initiator K2S2O8 was added slowly and the polymerization was allowed to proceed for 6 h. The obtained emulsion was washed with centrifugation using ethanol to acquire the polystyrene (PS) microspheres.
A drop of 5 mg/mL of sample solution was placed directly on the surface of PTFE film, PP film, poly(methyl methacrylate) sheet (PMMA), and glass sheet.

CA, fluoroalcohol, fatty alcohol, and mPEG were used as the starting materials for the preparation of water-soluble surface-active FH-CDs. CA was the carbon source, fluoroalcohol was used to endow the FH-CDs with high surface activities, fatty alcohol was to introduce lipophilic properties that might enhance the interfacial activity, and mPEG was used to introduce hydrophilic properties into the FH-CDs. It is well known that fluorosurfactants with longer fluorocarbon chains generally have higher surface activity; however, longer fluorocarbon chains also increase the fluorosurfactant toxicity [38]. For example, perfluorooctane surfactants with eight perfluorinated carbon atoms (C8) are extremely persistent in the environment [39], leading to them being categorized as persistent organic pollutants by the Stockholm Convention [40]. Currently, less toxic fluorosurfactants with six perfluorinated carbon atoms (C6) are the main products on the market [41–47], such as Capstone™ 1157 (C1157) and 1460 (C1460) from DuPont (Chemours) [38]. Hence, in this work, we adopted perfluorohexyl ethyl alcohol (F13-OH, C6 type) as the starting fluoroalcohol material. n-Hexanol, n-heptanol, and n-octanol were chosen as the fatty alcohols. Longer hydrocarbon chains were also explored; however, the resulting CDs were not fully soluble in water. Moreover, it was found that without the participation of mPEG, the resulting FH-CDs were not soluble in water, thus mPEG was used to increase their hydrophilicity.

CDs with CA as the carbon source are generally prepared via the hydrothermal technique [28], where water is used as the solvent. F13-OH and the adopted fatty alcohols are insoluble in water even at high temperature, thus the traditional one-pot hydrothermal technique is not suitable for preparing the target FH-CDs. DMF was chosen following a detailed investigation into appropriate co-solvents for the starting materials. All the starting materials and DMF were mixed in an autoclave and a one-pot solvothermal technique was adopted to prepare the target FH-CDs ( Scheme 1).

The solutions of FH-CDs were yellowish and they emitted cyan light under 365-nm UV lamp irradiation (inset in Fig. 1A). From Fig. 1 it is clear that both increasing the solvothermal reaction temperature and prolonging the reaction time can lead to a marked increase in the fluorescence intensity, which indicates that the adopted solvothermal conditions were suitable for preparing FH-CDs. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements showed that all the FH-CDs were smaller than 10 nm under the adopted solvothermal conditions (Fig. S1). DLS also showed that the higher the reaction temperature and the longer the reaction time were, the larger the FH-CDs were.

As the aim of this work was to prepare surface-active amphiphilic CDs, the influence of the preparation temperature and time on the surface activity of FH-CDs were studied. The obtained solid FH-CDs prepared under different temperature and time were diluted with deionized water to prepare a series of concentration (C) of aqueous solution of FH-CDs, and then the corresponding equilibrium surface tension (γ) were measured. Plots of γ versus log C were shown in Fig. 2. Initially, γ decreased sharply with increasing FH-CD concentration, and finally leveled off. The critical micelle concentration (CMC) and γ at CMC (γ
CMC ) can be read directly from the inflection points. The lower CMC and γ
CMC are, the higher the surface activity is. From Fig. 2 A (Fig. S2A and Fig. S3A) it is clear that FH-CD samples prepared at the solvothermal reaction temperature in the range 140–180 °C have a better surface activity. Thus, the lowest temperature in this range, 140 °C, was selected as the optimal reaction temperature. From Fig. 2B (Fig. S2B and Fig. S3B) it is clear that at 140 °C a 6-hour reaction is sufficient to achieve FH-CDs with the best surface activity. Hence, the optimized solvothermal conditions of 140 °C for 6 h was used to prepare the subsequent FH-CDs with different compositions, and the formulations for the FH-CDs are listed in Table 1.

The obtained FH-CDs were further characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy (NMR), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and gel permeation chromatography (GPC). Dark dots with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm can be clearly seen in the TEM image, indicating the successful carbonization of CA under the optimal solvothermal conditions ( Fig. 3A). Because the concentration of the carbon source is fixed, all the samples had an average diameter of approximately 1.6 nm. FH-CDs were further characterized by FT-IR (Fig. 3B). Compared with the FT-IR spectrum of pure CD from CA, a new band was observed at 1102 cm−1, which is assigned to the C-O stretching vibration of mPEG, while the multi-bands at 1293, 1244, and 1190 cm−1 are assigned to the C-F stretching vibration of polyfluorinated aliphatic compounds. The C-H stretching vibration bands of the alkyl chains were observed at 2927 and 2876 cm−1. The typical ester CO stretching vibration of pure CD occurs at 1723 cm−1, whereas that for the FH-CDs clearly shifted to 1738 cm−1, indicating the formation of a new kind of ester group between the carboxylic acid groups of CD and the hydroxyl groups of mPEG, fatty alcohol, and F13-OH.

The typical 1H NMR spectrum of FH-CD is shown in Fig. 3C. For clear assignment, we also prepared pure CD, mPEG modified CD (denoted PEG-CD), mPEG and fatty alcohol modified CD (denoted H-CD), and mPEG and F13-OH modified CD (denoted F-CD) for comparison (Fig. S4). The typical protons of mPEG and alkyl chains were observed and are assigned as shown in Fig. 3C. The characteristic new signals—compared with pure CD—between 4.1 and 4.4 ppm are due to the α protons of the ester groups, additionally verifying the ester linkage between the CD moieties and mPEG and hydrocarbon moieties. Although the proton signals of the fluorocarbon chains were not clearly observed in the spectra of F-CD and FH-CD, strong fluorine signals from the perfluoroalkyl chains were clearly observed in the corresponding 19F NMR spectrum (Fig. 3D). The intense signal at − 80.84 ppm is due to the CF3 group of the linear perfluoroalkyl chain. The higher chemical shifts between − 110 and − 130 ppm are attributed to the CF2 group of the perfluoroalkyl chains. Based on the integrals of these fluorine signals, it is clear that the perfluoroalkyl chains are intact during the solvothermal reaction.

The full XPS survey spectrum shows three prominent peaks assigned to C1s, O1s, and F1s ( Fig. 4A). In the high-resolution spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4B), the deconvoled peaks at 284.6, 285.8, 286.5, 289, and 291.6 eV are assigned to the methylene carbons not connected to heteroatoms, and carbons on ethers, esters, carboxylic acids, and CF2/CF3, respectively. The high-resolution O1s spectrum (Fig. 4C) contains two peaks at 532.1 and 533 eV, assigned to the ester and ether/alcohol, respectively. The high-resolution F1s spectrum again reveals the presence of CF2 and CF3 groups (Fig. 4D). The XPS results indicate that there is esterification of the fatty alcohol chains, PEG chains, and fluorocarbon chains with partial carboxyl groups in the citric acid carbon source, resulting in the obtained CDs being grafted with mPEG, hydrocarbon, and fluorocarbon chains.

It is known that the initial polycondensation among CA molecules and the subsequent dehydrogenation are the CD forming mechanism [48], which means CDs present some polymer characteristics [49]. Hence, all the FH-CDs were analyzed by GPC similarly to polymers (Fig. S5) and all the GPC profiles present a mono-modal signal. The M n and dispersity (Đ) of all the FH-CDs were interpreted using PMMA as the standards, and the results are summarized in Table 1. All the FH-CDs have similar M n values that fall in the range 1.0 × 103–1.3 × 103, indicating their similar size. Moreover, the dispersities (Đ) of all the samples are very narrow (Đ in the range 1.07–1.11), indicating that the adopted solvothermal conditions are suitable for preparing homogeneous FH-CD nanoparticles.

The storage stability of an aqueous solution of FH-CDs at room temperature was investigated by DLS (Fig. S6A). It was found that FH-CDs in water prefer to form several-hundred nanometer sized stable aggregates. The same phenomenon was observed for pure CDs that had not hydrophobic chains introduced (Fig. S6B), thus it was believed that the significant π-π interaction of CDs might contribute to the formation of large aggregates.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface and interfacial activities of FH-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The surface activity plots of γ versus log C for the as-prepared FH-CDs are shown in Fig. S7. These were used to determine the CMC and γ
CMC , which are shown in Fig. 5. Comparing FH-CD1, FH-CD2, and FH-CD3 shows that both the γ
CMC and CMC increase with increasing carbon number of the fatty alcohol (Fig. 5A). Comparing FH-CD4, FH-CD3, and FH-CD5 reveals that increasing the ratio of fluorocarbon chains to hydrocarbon chains enhances the surface activity, shown by the lower CMC and γ
CMC (Fig. 5B), which indicates the importance of fluorocarbon chains in the efficient improvement of surface activity. Subsequently, the surface activity of FH-CD was compared with those of pure CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, and F-CD (Fig. 5C). CD and PEG-CD had no obvious surface activity because of the lack of hydrophobic chains (Fig. S7C). Both H-CD and F-CD effectively reduced the surface tension of water; however, F-CD exhibited higher surface activity than H-CD. Because FH-CD bears both fluorocarbon chains and hydrocarbon chains, its surface activity lies between F-CD and H-CD (Fig. 5C). It should be noted that H-CD and other hydrocarbon surfactants can only reduce the surface tension of water to ∼25 mN/m, whereas F-CD and the optimized FH-CDs (FH-CD1, 2, 3, 4) reduced the surface tension of water below 20 mN/m. F-CD and FH-CD1 showed the lowest γ
CMC of 16.5 and 16.7 mN/m, respectively. The surface activity of FH-CD was further compared with those of the commercially available small molecule fluorosurfactant C1157 and polymeric fluorosurfactant C1460 with the same length fluorocarbon chain (Fig. 5D). It is clear that the surface activity of FH-CD is poorer than that of C1157, but better than that of the polymeric C1460. In addition, the influence of the M n of the mPEG moiety on the surface activity of FH-CDs was also investigated. Because the mPEG moiety in FH-CDs is only responsible for water solubility, no clear influence of mPEG moiety M n on surface activity was observed (Fig. S8).

Typical plots of γ -log C of FH-CD at different temperatures are shown in Fig. 6A. CMC, γ
CMC , and the surfactant concentration required to reduce the surface tension of water by 20 mN/m (C 20) were read directly from the plots. Based on these basic parameters, other physicochemical parameters including efficiency of adsorption (pC 20), effectiveness of the adsorption and micellization process (CMC/C 20), maximum surface excess concentration (Γ max) and minimum occupied area per molecule (A min), and standard free energies of micelle formation (ΔG
m ) and surface adsorption (ΔG
ad ), were calculated using the corresponding equations (see Supporting Information) and are summarized in Table 2. It is clear that increasing the temperature favors both the micelle-forming ability and adsorption tendency at the interface, which is reflected in the decreasing CMC, γ
CMC , ΔG
m , and ΔG
ad and the increasing pC 20 and CMC/C 20. This is primarily because the increase in temperature reduces the hydration of polar PEG moieties and promotes the formation of micelles. Moreover, increasing temperature leads to the gradual decrease of Γ max and the gradual increase of A min. The reason might be that the temperature increase accelerates the thermal motion of FH-CDs, which leads to a decrease in their arrangement density at the gas-liquid interface, thereby increasing the single-molecule area occupied by FH-CDs and reducing the saturation adsorption capacity. Fig. 6B shows ΔG
ad versus temperature, from which the entropy of adsorption (ΔS
ad ) was acquired from the negative slope value according to equation ∂ΔG
ad /∂T = -ΔS
ad , and the enthalpy of adsorption (ΔH
ad ) was calculated according to equation ΔH
ad = ΔG
ad +TΔS
ad . The negative ΔG
ad indicates that the micellization process of FH-CD surfactant in water is spontaneous and the positive ΔS
ad reflects the increased disorder of the system. The positive ΔH
ad implies that the absorption process is endothermic. Hence, the adsorption of FH-CDs at the air-solution interface is an entropy-driven process.

In addition to surface activity, interfacial activity is another important property that demonstrates the performance of surfactants. It is more complex than surface activity because of the variety of interface types. Here we only measured and compared the oil-water interfacial activities of the obtained FH-CDs, with cyclohexane used as the oil component. From Fig. 7 it is clear that increasing the concentration leads to a marked decrease in the oil-water interfacial tensions (γ o/w) until it levels off. FH-CDs with longer hydrocarbon chains showed better interfacial activity, indicated by the lower γ o/w value (Fig. 7A). From Fig. 7B it can be seen that a balanced ratio of fluorocarbon to hydrocarbon chains is effective at lowering γ o/w. Subsequently, the interfacial activity of FH-CD was compared with those of H-CD and F-CD. From Fig. 7C it is clear that F-CD, without hydrocarbon chains, is more efficient for reducing γ o/w in the low concentration range, whereas H-CD, without fluorocarbon chains, is more efficient for reducing γ o/w in the high concentration range. H-CD reduced γ o/w markedly more than F-CD, indicating that H-CD has a better oil-water interfacial activity than F-CD. Notably, FH-CD did not exhibit an interfacial activity between F-CD and H-CD, and contrarily it inherited the merits of both F-CD and H-CD, exhibiting good interfacial activity over the whole concentration range. The interfacial activity of FH-CD was further compared with those of commercially available C1157 and C1460 (Fig. 7D). FH-CD showed better interfacial activity than C1157 and C1460 in the high concentration range. FH-CD reduced γ o/w to ∼2.2 mN/m, whereas C1157 and C1460 only reduced γ o/w to ∼6.4 and 7.0 mN/m, respectively.

The excellent surface and interface activities of FH-CDs can be tentatively explained as follows: The high surface activity is attributed to the fluorine atoms in FH-CDs that have lower polarizability and higher ionization potential, so that the van der Waals interactions between the fluorocarbon chains are weaker, and the tension necessary for FH-CDs to move from the aqueous solution to the surface of the solution is small. Hence, numerous FH-CDs aggregated on the solution surface, forming strong surface adsorption. Fluorocarbon chains not only have a low affinity for water, but also have a low affinity for oil, thus exhibiting both hydrophobic and oleophobic properties, which is why the fluorocarbon chain has little effect on the interfacial tension at the oil-water interface. However, when fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon chains are combined, the fluorocarbon chain can selectively adsorb on the surface of water to reduce the surface tension, while the hydrocarbon chain can adsorb on the oil-water interface to reduce the interfacial tension [50,51]. FH-CD can therefore have excellent surface activity and interfacial activity at the same time.

The emulsifying ability of the obtained FH-CD was tested. The binary mixture of cyclohexane and water was used as an emulsifying target. The addition of FH-CD into the binary mixture formed a delicate W/O emulsion in the cyclohexane layer after shaking for one min ( Fig. 8A) and no demulsification occurred even after one week, indicating the emulsifying ability of the obtained FH-CD. For comparison, the emulsifying abilities of pure CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, and F-CD were also determined. It was found that pure CD and PEG-CD had almost no emulsifying ability, whereas H-CD and F-CD formed a stable thicker and thinner emulsion layer than FH-CD, respectively. This indicates that both hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon chains play a major role in the emulsion formation, but hydrocarbon chains have a greater contribution than fluorocarbon chains. The optical microscopy images of the emulsion layers showed that only sparse particles were present in the very thin emulsion layers with the hydrophilic pure CD or PEG-CD as an emulsifier, whereas densely-packed particles were present in the thick emulsion layers with H-CD, F-CD, or FH-CD as the emulsifier. The average-size of particles was in the order H-CD < FH-CD < F-CD, further indicating the greater contribution of the hydrocarbon chains to the emulsifying ability compared with that of the fluorocarbon chains. Photoluminescent layers around the emulsion particles could be clearly observed under fluorescence microscopy owing to the presence of the fluorescent FH-CDs (Fig. S9). Zeta potential measurement showed that all the emulsifiers, H-CD, F-CD, and FH-CD, resulted in emulsion particles with strongly negative charge (Fig. S10).

The emulsifying ability of FH-CD led to its selection for the emulsion polymerization. A traditional styrene emulsion polymerization was attempted. Using FH-CD as the emulsifier and K2S2O8 as an initiator, a stable emulsion of PS was obtained (inset of Fig. 9A). Numerous dark dots were observed under the optical microscope (Fig. 9A), which emitted fluorescence under UV excitation (inset of Fig. 9A), indicating that the obtained PS latexes were capped by the fluorescent FH-CDs. The SEM images (Fig. 9B and 9C) clearly show that relatively uniform and smooth PS spheres were formed with an average size of approximately 1 µm. In the EDS images, in addition to densely-packed C (Fig. 9D), sparse O (Fig. 9E) and F (Fig. 9F) can also be observed, indicating the effective fusion of FH-CD with the formed PS latexes.

For comparison, the emulsion polymerization of styrene was also carried out without emulsifier or with pure CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, or F-CD as the emulsifier with a concentration of 15 mg/mL. The corresponding SEM images and emulsion photographs are shown in Fig. 10 (Figs. S11 and S12). It was found that all the emulsions were sufficiently stable except the emulsifier-free conditions. The SEM images show that the PS latexes obtained with pure CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, or F-CD as the emulsifier were not as uniform as those obtained using FH-CD as the emulsifier. Moreover, the PS latexes prepared with H-CD as the emulsifier were not completely spherical. These control experiments show that there is a synergy of fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon chains in controlling the uniformity and morphology of PS latexes.

Emulsions stored at room temperature were further characterized by DLS following storage (Fig. S13). After storing for one week at room temperature, the emulsion with FH-CD as the emulsifier showed the greatest stability.

Finally, the wetting ability of the obtained FH-CD was tested on the surfaces of glass, PMMA, PP, and PTFE. The wetting ability was reflected by the contact angle, where a smaller contact angle represents a better wetting ability. For comparison, the contact angles of aqueous solutions of CD, PEG-CD, H-CD, and F-CD were also measured. Fig. 11 shows that the aqueous solutions of hydrophilic CD and PEG-CD only had good wetting abilities on the glass surface. This might be because the glass surface is also hydrophilic because of the presence of numerous silanol groups. Although PMMA possesses many polar ester groups, its surface is not hydrophilic, leading to the aqueous solutions of hydrophilic CD and PEG-CD showing poor wetting. The PP surface is extremely hydrophobic owing to its neat non-polar hydrocarbon composition, while the PTFE surface is both hydrophobic and oleophobic. Thus, the aqueous solutions of hydrophilic CD and PEG-CD did not wet them even with increasing concentration. Because F-CD and FH-CD can lower the surface tension of water, their aqueous solutions could wet all the surfaces. However, owing to the amphiphobic nature of the PTFE surface, their wetting of that surface was not as effective as for other surfaces. Increasing the concentration of FH-CD or prolonging the contacting time (Fig. S14) did however lead to excellent wetting even on the PTFE surface. The surface and interfacial activities of H-CD were lower than that of FH-CD, thus it can wet glass, PMMA, and PP effectively, but not the commonly non-wettable PTFE.

High-quality homogeneous amphiphilic CDs were prepared using the facile one-pot solvothermal technique. Integrating both fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hydrophobic chains into the CDs ensured both excellent surface and interfacial activities, and simultaneously reduced the γ
CMC and oil-water interfacial tension to 16.7 mN/m and 2.2 mN/m, respectively, which are uncommon characteristics among current amphiphilic nanoparticles (see Table S1). The fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains in CDs contributed to the excellent interfacial activity, while only the fluorocarbon chains contributed to the excellent surface activity. The surface and interfacial activities made the as-prepared amphiphilic CDs not only good emulsifiers, but also good wetting agents, even for extremely low-energy surfaces.

Until now, the types of amphiphilic CDs have remained limited. The one-pot solvothermal technique is a promising method for developing more amphiphilic CDs comprising not only fluorocarbon and hydrocarbon hybrid chains, but also other functional groups, which might further improve their performances or widen their application range, including for firefighting and in petrochemicals.

Chen Yu: Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Wang Yan: Methodology, Writing – original draft. Zhuang Shuang: Funding acquisition. Chen Wei-Guang: Data curation. Lu Lei: Investigation. Li Rong-Zhen: Formal analysis. Chen Pei-Yao: Conceptualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2022YFC3004900). We thank Liwen Bianji (Edanz) (www.liwenbianji.cn) for editing the English text of a draft of this manuscript.
